requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must establish and maintain properly structured accountable reimbursement plan allowing tax-free reimbursement of ordinary and necessary business expenses incurred by employees with appropriate substantiation requirements
criteria: Expenses must have business connection - incurred while performing services as employee
Must adequately account for expenses with receipts, documentation, and business purpose within reasonable time period
Must return any excess reimbursement or advances to employer within reasonable time (120 days)
Reimbursements must be for ordinary and necessary business expenses deductible by employer
Cannot reimburse personal, living, or family expenses even if incurred during business travel
Written plan document recommended documenting accountable plan requirements and procedures
Common reimbursable expenses: mileage, travel, meals (50% limit applies to employer), supplies, professional development
Contemporaneous substantiation required showing amount, time, place, business purpose, and business relationship

accountable_plan_requirements.tax_free_to_employee: Properly substantiated reimbursements excluded from employee W-2 wages and not subject to payroll taxes
accountable_plan_requirements.employer_deduction: Employer deducts reimbursed expenses as business expenses (subject to specific limitations like 50% meals)
accountable_plan_requirements.reasonable_time_period: Generally 60 days for substantiation and 120 days for returning excess advances
accountable_plan_requirements.substantiation_requirements: Receipt threshold generally $75 for non-lodging; lower for lodging; business purpose always required
accountable_plan_requirements.per_diem_methods: Can use IRS per diem rates for meals and lodging simplifying substantiation requirements
accountable_plan_requirements.nonaccountable_vs_accountable: Nonaccountable reimbursements treated as taxable wages subject to payroll tax and withholding

restrictions: Failure to meet accountable plan requirements causes reimbursements to become taxable wages on W-2
Employees cannot deduct unreimbursed business expenses after TCJA eliminated miscellaneous itemized deductions (through 2025)
Plan cannot discriminate in favor of highly compensated employees for fringe benefit purposes
Excess reimbursements not returned within 120 days treated as wages subject to payroll taxes retroactively
Recordkeeping requirements strict; must maintain supporting documentation for statute of limitations period
Mileage reimbursement above IRS standard rate may be taxable income to extent of excess
Cell phone, home office, and other mixed-use items require allocation between business and personal use
State tax treatment generally follows federal accountable plan rules but verify state-specific requirements
